assesment:
Actinic Keratosis
 Discussed with patient that these are considered to be precancerous and are primarily caused by long-term exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun but can be caused from other sources. Management options were discussed, including: cryotherapy, topical therapies (5FU, imiquimod, etc.). Risks and benefits of each reviewed, with the primary goal of treatment to prevent the progression of the lesion. Patient educated on the importance of sun protection.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment: 
 
- Cryotherapy done today. 
- Discussed cryotherapy procedure with patient prior to treatment. Potential risk factors associated with cryotherapy includes, but is not limited to: temporary pain or discomfort during the freezing process, skin discoloration/pigmentary changes, scarring, and recurrence of lesions. Patient is aware that more than one treatment may be needed. Patient agrees to treatment with liquid nitrogen today. 
- Advised patient that site will become tender, irritated and may come off. Patient is aware that additional treatments may be needed. If lesion does not resolve in 4-6 weeks, return to clinic.

assesment:
Rosacea; not at treatment goal
 Management options were discussed, including: systemic therapy, including oral antibiotics, sulfa wash, and topical therapy. 
Risks and benefits of all options were reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with: 
 
-Apply ivermectin once daily to the affected areas. - refilled- Discussed goals of using Soolantra with patient, including reducing inflammation and redness associated with Rosacea. Patient reports no allergies to Soolantra (ivermectin). Advised patient that Soolantra should only be used on clean, dry skin. Most common side effects including but not limited to: irritation, dryness, itching and mild burning at the application site reviewed with patient. Soolantra should not be applied near the eyes and hands should be washed after application. Advised patient to use sunscreen and protective clothings to avoid sunburn as the medication can make the skin more sensitive to sunlight. 
Seborrheic Keratosis - Inflamed
 Considering the patient’s history and examination, the diagnosis is most
 consistent with inflamed seborrheic keratosis with pruritus. 
Management options discussed with patient: cryotherapy vs. surgical removal. Risks and benefits of all management options were discussed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following:
 
- Cryotherapy done today. 
- Discussed cryotherapy procedure with patient prior to treatment. Potential risk factors associated with cryotherapy includes, but is not limited to: temporary pain or discomfort during the freezing process, skin discoloration/pigmentary changes, scarring, and recurrence of lesions. Patient is aware that more than one treatment may be needed. Patient agrees to treatment with liquid nitrogen today. 
- Advised patient that site will become tender, irritated and may come off. Patient is aware that additional treatments may be needed. If lesion does not resolve in 4-6 weeks, return to clinic.
 
Aesthetics - Wrinkles; laxity and dullness
- recommend for patient to seek consultation with plastics
- I advise patient to return to clinic for skin maintenance. Recommended patient to start a vitamin C daily in the morning with SPF use throughout the day.
- patient agrees, plastic surgeon, recommendations provided.

Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
- Biopsy results reviewed with patient. Patient acknowledges understanding.
Recommend Mohs vs SRT. Patient states she opts for Cryotherapy, extensively discussed with patient risks of cryotherapy treatment for SSCIS. PVU. 
Cryotherapy done today. Advised patient that site will become tender, irritated and may come off. Patient is aware that additional treatments may be needed. If lesion does not resolve in 4-6 weeks, return to clinic.

All questions answered in full. Risks, benefits, alternatives, side effects discussed. Return to clinic precautions reviewed. Patient agrees with plan and verbalizes understanding.

Squamous Cell Carcinoma; improving/not at treatment goal
Considering the patients history, examination and histopathology, the diagnosis is most consistent with Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
Management options discussed with patient: Mohs micrographic surgery, ED&C, excisional surgery, and cryosurgery. Risk and benefits of each option reviewed.
Patient opted to proceed with the following treatment:
- Cryotherapy done today. 
- Discussed cryotherapy procedure with patient prior to treatment. Potential risk factors associated with cryotherapy includes, but is not limited to: temporary pain or discomfort during the freezing process, skin discoloration/pigmentary changes, scarring, and recurrence of lesions. Patient is aware that more than one treatment may be needed. Patient agrees to treatment with liquid nitrogen today. 
- Advised patient that site will become tender, irritated and may come off. Patient is aware that additional treatments may be needed. If lesion does not resolve in 4-6 weeks, return to clinic.

Discussed diagnosis, treatment options for the lesion with patient including, surgical removal, mohs surgery and malignant destruction with ED&C or cryotherapy.
Patient agreed to proceed with destruction of the malignant lesion.  Potential risks associated with procedure including bleeding, risk of infection, scarring, nerve damage at site, tumor recurrence, and allergic reaction reviewed with patient. 
The patient tolerated the procedure well. Wound instructions were given to the patient. Patient advised about postprocedural wound healing and expectations.

procedure:
-Discussed cryotherapy procedure with patient for actinic keratosis. Potential risk factors associated with cryotherapy includes, but is not limited to: temporary pain or discomfort during the freezing process, skin discoloration/pigmentary changes, scarring, and recurrence of lesions. biopsyNotes:
A. Biopsy
Clinical Diagnosis: ISK 
Location: Right Back
Method: Shave
 The patient gave informed consent for this procedure. After consent was obtained, attention was turned toward the area of interest which was disinfected and cleaned with alcohol. Local anesthetic, 2% lidocaine with epinephrine was infiltrated around the area of interest. The superficial layers of the site were removed by Shave method using a Number 15. The specimen was placed in a bottle labeled A. #WC26-353.
Pressure was applied to achieve hemostasis. Bacitracin was applied at the wound site and it was bandaged. Patient tolerated the procedure well and wound care instructions were reviewed and given to the patient.
